did_this_paper_use_ai_to_obtain_results_.details: The paper used an Extreme Learning Machine (ELM), a single-hidden layer feed-forward neural network, for the classification of dermoscopic images into benign and malignant melanoma. It also employed computer vision techniques including Non-Subsampled Contourlet Transform (NSCT) and Eig(Hess) histogram of oriented gradients (HOG) for feature extraction, and adaptive thresholding with region growing for lesion segmentation.

is_this_paper_a_review_paper_or_a_resear.details: The paper presents original experimental work involving the development of a computer-aided diagnosis (CAD) system for automated melanoma recognition. It details specific methods including lesion segmentation using adaptive thresholding and region growing, feature extraction via NSCT and Eig(Hess) HOG, and classification using an Extreme Learning Machine (ELM). The authors report original results from experiments conducted on the ISIC benchmark dataset, including segmentation rankings (Jaccard Score) and classification metrics (Accuracy, Sensitivity, Specificity), which characterizes it as a research paper rather than a review.

title: Automated Melanoma Recognition in Dermoscopic Images Based on Extreme Learning Machine (ELM)
brief_summary: This study developed a computer-aided diagnosis system for automated melanoma recognition in dermoscopic images to address the subjectivity and time consumption of manual diagnosis. The proposed method utilized adaptive thresholding combined with multiscale region growing for lesion segmentation, followed by feature extraction using Non-Subsampled Contourlet Transform (NSCT) and Eig(Hess) histogram of oriented gradients (HOG). These features were classified as benign or malignant using an Extreme Learning Machine (ELM) with 700 hidden neurons and a tangent-sigmoid activation function. The system's effectiveness was evaluated on the International Skin Imaging Collaboration (ISIC) benchmark dataset, which included 900 training and 379 test images. The approach achieved a Jaccard Score of 0.822 for segmentation, ranking third among 28 teams, and demonstrated superior classification performance with an accuracy of 0.895 and specificity of 0.980 on the test set.

lesion_types_studied.details: The primary focus of the study is automated recognition of melanoma. The classification task distinguishes between benign and malignant lesions, with malignant corresponding to melanoma. Approximately 30.3% of the dataset (273 images in the training set) was malignant.

The study classifies lesions into two classes: benign and malignant. The term 'benign' is used as the counterpart to malignant melanoma. A 'dysplastic nevus' is explicitly mentioned in Figure 1 and Figure 3 captions as an example of a lesion segmented in the study.

all_task_types.details: The paper focuses on a two-class lesion classification task (benign vs. malignant) using an Extreme Learning Machine (ELM). The model predicts discrete diagnostic categories. Although the challenge normalized prediction scores into confidence intervals from 0.0 (benign) to 1.0 (malignant), the primary task is binary classification, evaluated using accuracy, sensitivity, and specificity.

The paper also addresses automatic lesion segmentation to detect lesions from background skin. This was evaluated using the Jaccard Score (0.822) in a competition, ranking 3rd among 28 teams. However, the primary research contribution and detailed experimental results focus on the classification task.

datasets_used.name: International Skin Imaging Collaboration (ISIC) Benchmark Dataset
datasets_used.description: A benchmark dataset from the ISIC data archive containing dermoscopic images collected from leading clinical centers internationally. The study utilized data from the 'Skin Lesion Analysis Towards Melanoma Detection' challenge hosted at the 2016 International Symposium on Biomedical Imaging (ISBI’2016). Ground truth was generated by a panel of dermoscopic experts.

use.training: Used for training the segmentation algorithm and for parameter optimization of the ELM classifier (manually divided into training and validation sets for this purpose).
use.validation: Manually divided from the original training dataset by the authors for parameter optimization of the ELM classifier (specifically determining the number of hidden neurons and activation function).
use.testing: Used to evaluate the classification performance (accuracy, sensitivity, specificity) of the proposed ELM system. The authors note they did not officially participate in the classification task submission but evaluated their results on this test set after ground truth was provided.
use.other: Used for segmentation task evaluation in the ISBI 2016 challenge, where the system ranked 3rd among 28 teams based on Jaccard Score.

statistical_methods_used.purpose: Used to evaluate the effect of the number of hidden nodes (L) on training and testing accuracies during the parameter optimization of the Extreme Learning Machine (ELM).
Used as the primary metric to rank participants in the ISBI 2016 segmentation challenge and to evaluate segmentation performance.

statistical_methods_used.method: Pixel-level accuracy, pixel-level sensitivity, pixel-level specificity, Dice Coefficient
statistical_methods_used.purpose: Used to compare segmentation submissions in the ISBI 2016 challenge.

hypothesis_testing.interpretation: The paper did not report any formal hypothesis testing, p-values, or significance tests. Performance was evaluated using descriptive metrics such as Jaccard Score, Accuracy, Sensitivity, and Specificity, and results were compared via ranking in a competition.

ISIC Benchmark Dataset (ISBI 2016 Challe.notes: The proposed system ranked 3rd among 28 participating teams in the segmentation task. The ranking was based on the Jaccard Index. Other metrics used for comparison in the challenge included pixel-level accuracy, pixel-level sensitivity, pixel-level specificity, and Dice Coefficient.

ISIC Benchmark Dataset (Classification T.notes: The authors did not officially participate in the classification task submission but evaluated their results on the test set of 379 images after ground truth was provided. The approach achieved the best accuracy among the listed participants and specificity comparable to the highest reported (Jordan Yap). Rankings in the official challenge were based on average precision evaluated between sensitivity of 0-100%.

architecture_details.weight_initialization: Randomly assigned input weights and biases for hidden nodes
architecture_details.output_weight_calculation: Analytically defined using the least square method (Moore-Penrose generalized inverse)
architecture_details.feature_input: Combined feature vector consisting of NSCT features (156-D), Eig(Hess) HOG features (128 elements), shape features (9-D), mass features (7-D), and GLCM texture features (6-D)

training_details.validation_method: 10-fold cross validation (used to determine optimal number of hidden nodes)

preprocessing_techniques: Conversion of color images to grayscale
Adaptive thresholding based on color component entropy
Morphological operations (erosion, opening with 5x5 square structuring element, and closing)
Region growing to remove corners and segment lesions
Non-Subsampled Contourlet Transform (NSCT) decomposition (4-level pyramidal decomposition)
Eig(Hess) Histogram of Oriented Gradients (HOG) extraction using Hessian matrix eigenvalues
Gray Level Co-occurrence Matrix (GLCM) texture feature extraction (6-D)
conclusions: The proposed computer-aided diagnosis system, utilizing adaptive thresholding, multiscale region growing, NSCT, Eig(Hess) HOG features, and an Extreme Learning Machine (ELM), achieved a Jaccard Score of 0.822 for lesion segmentation, ranking 3rd among 28 teams in the ISBI 2016 challenge.

The ELM classifier achieved an accuracy of 0.895 and a specificity of 0.980 on the ISIC test set, demonstrating superior accuracy compared to other top-performing participants in the classification task.

The study highlights the potential of the proposed CAD system for real clinical integration and benefiting biomedical information systems due to its effective feature extraction and classification capabilities.

The authors acknowledge that while the technology shows promise, computer-aided diagnosis systems are still in the early development stage, characterized by a lack of descriptive feature generation and benchmark evaluation in ground-truth datasets.
The system's performance was evaluated on a benchmark dataset (ISIC) with ground truth generated by a panel of dermoscopic experts, validating its effectiveness in a realistic comparison setting.
important_remarks: The authors did not officially participate in the classification task of the ISBI 2016 challenge due to time constraints; classification results were evaluated post-hoc on the test set after ground truth was provided, which limits the comparability with official challenge submissions.
The classification model exhibits low sensitivity (0.453) despite high specificity (0.980) and accuracy (0.895), indicating a high rate of false negatives for melanoma detection.
The study lacks formal statistical hypothesis testing, p-values, or confidence intervals to support the reported performance metrics.
The dataset is imbalanced, with only approximately 30.3% of the training images (273 out of 900) being malignant, which may bias the model towards the benign class.

The validation set for hyperparameter tuning (number of hidden neurons and activation function) was manually divided from the original training dataset by the authors, rather than using a pre-defined external validation split.
The paper acknowledges that computer-aided diagnosis systems are still in the early development stage with a lack of descriptive feature generation and benchmark evaluation in ground-truth datasets.

The segmentation performance (Jaccard Score 0.822) ranked 3rd among 28 teams, but the classification performance was not ranked in the official challenge, relying instead on a retrospective comparison with top participants.
